description: US domestic violence and stalking protective orders: order types, evidence standards, firearm surrender, interstate enforcement, and safety planning including technology safety. Verified 2026.
source: VAWA 18 U.S.C. §§ 2265-2266, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(8)-(9), U.S. v. Rahimi (2024), state protective order statutes, National Network to End Domestic Violence Safety Net project, National DV Hotline

triples.subject: protective order
triples.object: civil court order restraining an abuser from contact, proximity, or specific conduct; called protection order, restraining order, or injunction depending on state

triples.subject: emergency protective order (EPO)
triples.object: issued immediately, often by police calling an on-call judge at the scene; lasts days (e.g., 5-7 days) to bridge until the victim can petition the court

triples.subject: temporary (ex parte) order
triples.object: issued the day of filing based on the petition alone, without the respondent present; typically lasts about 14 days (up to 20-25 in some states) until the full hearing

triples.subject: final protective order
triples.object: issued after a hearing where both sides can appear; typically lasts 1-5 years, and many states allow renewal or permanent orders for severe cases

triples.subject: protective order evidence standard
triples.object: civil preponderance of the evidence (more likely than not) in most states — far lower than criminal beyond-a-reasonable-doubt; no criminal charge or police report is required to petition

triples.subject: protective order evidence
triples.object: petitioner testimony alone can suffice; texts, voicemails, photos of injuries, medical records, witness statements, and call logs strengthen the case — bring printouts, not just a phone

triples.subject: protective order filing
triples.object: free — VAWA conditions federal funds on states not charging filing or service fees for DV protective orders (34 U.S.C. § 10450)

triples.subject: full faith and credit
triples.object: a valid protective order is enforceable in every state, territory, and tribal land (18 U.S.C. § 2265); carry a certified copy but registration in the new state is not required

triples.subject: firearm prohibition
triples.object: 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(8) bars firearm possession while subject to a qualifying final protective order (issued after notice and hearing, protecting an intimate partner or child); upheld in U.S. v. Rahimi (2024)

triples.subject: firearm surrender
triples.object: many states (e.g., CA, WA, NY, IL, MD) require respondents to surrender firearms to police or a dealer within 24-72 hours and file proof; ex parte orders trigger surrender in some states but not under the federal statute

triples.subject: DV misdemeanor conviction
triples.object: 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(9) (Lautenberg Amendment) permanently bars firearm possession after a misdemeanor crime of domestic violence conviction

triples.subject: protective order violation
triples.object: criminal offense (misdemeanor, felony for repeats) and/or contempt; most states have mandatory or preferred arrest policies for violations — call 911 and report every violation to build the record

triples.subject: protective order
triples.object: only the respondent can violate the order; a petitioner who initiates contact does not commit a crime but weakens enforcement — modification through the court is the safe route to resume contact

triples.subject: stalking protective order
triples.object: most states offer civil stalking or harassment orders that do not require any intimate-partner or family relationship, covering neighbors, acquaintances, and strangers

triples.subject: protective order relief
triples.object: no-contact and stay-away distances, exclusive use of the home, temporary custody and child support, pet protection, and no third-party contact

triples.subject: lethality indicator: strangulation
triples.object: prior non-fatal strangulation raises later homicide risk roughly 7-fold (Glass et al. 2008); always disclose it to court and medical providers — many states charge it as a felony

triples.subject: lethality indicators
triples.object: abuser access to a gun (about 5x homicide risk, Campbell 2003), recent separation, threats to kill, unemployment, and escalating violence — the Danger Assessment tool scores these

triples.subject: safety plan
triples.object: concrete, personalized plan for staying safer while in, leaving, or after leaving an abusive situation; leaving is the highest-risk period, so plans focus heavily on the exit

triples.subject: go-bag
triples.object: IDs, birth certificates, Social Security cards, protective order copies, medications, cash, spare keys, phone charger, kids' comfort items — stored with a trusted person or hidden outside the home

triples.subject: National Domestic Violence Hotline
triples.object: 1-800-799-7233, text START to 88788, or chat at thehotline.org; 24/7, confidential, can locate local shelters and help build a safety plan

triples.subject: technology safety: phone
triples.object: assume a shared or abuser-purchased phone is monitored; check for stalkerware (unexplained battery drain, unknown device-admin apps), and do sensitive research on a library or friend's device

triples.subject: technology safety: location
triples.object: audit location sharing (Find My, Google Maps sharing, family plans), scan for hidden trackers like AirTags (iPhone alerts automatically; Android users can scan via Find My Device 'unknown tracker alerts'), and check vehicles

triples.subject: technology safety: accounts
triples.object: create a new email the abuser doesn't know, change passwords from a safe device, enable two-factor, and leave shared iCloud/Google family groups; iPhone Safety Check (Settings > Privacy) resets sharing in one flow

triples.subject: technology safety: evidence
triples.object: preserve threats before blocking — screenshot with dates and phone numbers visible, forward to a safe email; most states allow recording only with consent rules that vary, so check before recording calls

triples.subject: address confidentiality program (ACP)
triples.object: state-run substitute mailing address so survivors' real addresses stay out of public records (voter rolls, DMV, court filings); available in roughly 40+ states via the attorney general or secretary of state

triples.subject: protective orders and immigration
triples.object: immigration status does not bar getting a protective order; VAWA self-petitions and U-visas exist for abused immigrants — courts should not ask petitioners about status

triples.subject: protective order denial
triples.object: denial of a temporary order does not end the case — the petitioner can still proceed to the full hearing, refile after new incidents, or appeal; documentation of each incident matters
